title: Beyond blood pressure: a comprehensive overview of clinical indices in shock and tissue hypoperfusion
one_line_summary: This review outlines a multidimensional framework for hemodynamic assessment in shock, moving beyond macrocirculatory parameters to include microcirculatory, metabolic, and emerging biomarkers, advocating for a physiology-guided, stepwise resuscitation strategy.
key_pearls: Target MAP >= 65 mm Hg in initial shock resuscitation, but recognize this alone is insufficient for tissue perfusion.
Avoid over-resuscitation; keep CVP low to maintain systemic perfusion pressure (MAP - CVP) and protect renal function.
Assess peripheral perfusion using Capillary Refill Time (CRT > 3 seconds) and Skin Mottling Score to detect persistent tissue hypoperfusion despite normal MAP.
A P(v-a)CO2 gap > 6 mm Hg indicates inadequate cardiac output for CO2 washout, requiring flow optimization (e.g., inotropes).
An ScvO2 > 80% with high lactate suggests microvascular shunt or mitochondrial failure; avoid aggressive fluid loading in this scenario.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology of Shock: Macro vs. Microcirculation
sections.content: In macrocirculatory shock, compensatory mechanisms preserve MAP and CO, but when MAP falls below the autoregulatory threshold, local flow becomes pressure-dependent. Microcirculatory shock is driven by ischemia, hypoxia, and inflammation, leading to: - Degradation of the endothelial glycocalyx and direct endothelial injury. - Increased vascular permeability, capillary leakage, and tissue edema, increasing oxygen diffusion distance. - Impaired red blood cell deformability and alignment. - Leukocyte adhesion, plasma extravasation, and microthrombosis causing capillary obstruction. - Loss of local flow autoregulation, diminished capillary recruitment, and heterogeneous perfusion. This leads to hemodynamic incoherence, where normal macrocirculatory parameters (e.g., MAP >= 65 mm Hg) do not guarantee microcirculatory flow restoration, resulting in persistent cellular hypoxia.

sections.heading: Conventional Macrocirculatory Parameters
sections.content: Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP) is targeted at >= 65 mm Hg, but it is an inadequate surrogate for tissue perfusion. Cardiac Output (CO) and Cardiac Index (CI) are linked to global oxygen delivery, but no single target exists; they must be interpreted in the context of DO2-VO2 mismatch. Central Venous Pressure (CVP) historically guided fluid resuscitation but carries a high risk of over-resuscitation. Elevated CVP reduces the pressure gradient for organ perfusion (Systemic Perfusion Pressure = MAP - CVP) and impairs renal venous outflow, increasing AKI risk.

sections.heading: Downstream Markers of Tissue Hypoperfusion and Organ Dysfunction
sections.content: Serum Lactate, with a cutoff > 2 mmol/L, is a primary biomarker for resuscitation adequacy, indicating anaerobic metabolism, but it is a lagging indicator and can be elevated by non-hypoxic processes. Venous Oxygen Saturation (SvO2 / ScvO2), normally 65-80%, reflects DO2-VO2 balance; low ScvO2 (< 65%) indicates inadequate DO2 or excessive VO2, while high ScvO2 (> 80%) in late shock suggests impaired cellular oxygen extraction or mitochondrial failure. The Venous-to-Arterial Carbon Dioxide Difference (P(v-a)CO2 gap) > 6 mm Hg indicates insufficient systemic blood flow and responds rapidly to perfusion changes. Clinical signs like oliguria and altered mental status are rapid, non-specific bedside indicators of hypoperfusion.

sections.heading: Clinical Signs of Microcirculatory Dysfunction
sections.content: Capillary Refill Time (CRT) is a rapid bedside test; a prolonged CRT > 3 seconds is linked to increased mortality. The ANDROMEDA-SHOCK trial showed CRT-targeted resuscitation (targeting CRT <= 3 seconds) was non-inferior to lactate-guided therapy. Skin Mottling Score (0-5) assesses patchy skin discoloration, with high scores independently associated with 28-day mortality in septic and cardiogenic shock. Core-Peripheral Temperature Gradients, a widened difference between core and peripheral temperature, indicate severe peripheral vasoconstriction and low cardiac output.

sections.heading: Device-Based Microcirculation Monitoring
sections.content: Handheld Vital Microscopy (HVM) uses SDF or IDF imaging to directly visualize sublingual microcirculation, quantifying capillary density and perfusion heterogeneity. Near-Infrared Spectroscopy (NIRS) non-invasively estimates tissue oxygen saturation (StO2) and, with a vascular occlusion test, provides dynamic data on microvascular reactivity. Contrast-Enhanced Ultrasound (CEUS) uses microbubble contrast to assess real-time, organ-level microvascular perfusion, with decreased renal cortical perfusion on CEUS correlating with severe AKI.

sections.heading: Emerging Biomarkers of Shock
sections.content: Emerging biomarkers include Endothelial & Glycocalyx Markers: - Syndecan-1, Heparan Sulfate, Hyaluronic Acid, and Soluble VE-Cadherin indicate glycocalyx degradation and barrier disruption. - Sphingosine-1-Phosphate (S1P) is protective. - A high Angiopoietin-2 to Angiopoietin-1 Ratio (Ang-2/Ang-1) is prognostic for sepsis severity. - Proadrenomedullin (pro-ADM) reflects endothelial dysfunction and vasoplegia. Inflammatory Biomarkers like IL-6, IL-8, TNF-alpha, and suPAR reflect inflammation and predict outcomes. Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone System (RAAS) markers, particularly high renin levels and a high renin/aldosterone ratio, correlate with vasopressor requirements and mortality. End-Organ Damage Biomarkers include: - Renal: NGAL and KIM-1 for early AKI. - Vascular/Metabolic: Circulating dipeptidyl peptidase 3 (cDPP3) for myocardial instability. - Hepatic/Intestinal: L-FABP for hepatic ischemia and I-FABP for enterocyte injury.

strengths_limitations: Strengths include providing an integrated physiological framework bridging macrohemodynamics and microvascular flow, highlighting both low-cost bedside tools (CRT, mottling score) and advanced technologies. Limitations are that many advanced monitoring tools and novel biomarkers lack standardized protocols, are operator-dependent, or are not widely available for routine clinical use.
